title: How I Got 2x More From My AI Coding Tools (After Understanding How They Actually Work)
author: Kayra Berk Tuncer
text:
How I Got 2x More From My AI Coding Tools (After Understanding How They Actually Work)
6 months ago, I was mass of AI tools that I don’t fully understand.
Cursor one day. Windsurf the next. Gemini for some things. Claude when I felt like it. Antigravity when someone recommended it.
I kept switching tools thinking the grass was greener.
Then I dug into how these tools actually work under the hood. Not the marketing. The actual mechanics.
What I found changed everything about how I use them.
I’ve been a developer at a large tech company for 5 years. This is what I wish someone had told me on day one.
Why Your AI Responses Get Slower (And Dumber) Over Time
I used to have 50-message conversations. By message 30, two things happened:
- Responses got slower
- The model started “forgetting” things I told it earlier
I blamed the model. “Claude is getting worse.” “They’re throttling me.”
Then I understood what was actually happening.
Every message in a conversation resends the ENTIRE history to the model. Message 1 sends 50 tokens. Message 20 sends 2,000+ tokens — all the previous context, again.
The model isn’t forgetting. It’s drowning.
Research shows that after ~3,000 tokens of context, reasoning quality starts dropping. By 10,000 tokens, the model mostly focuses on the last 2,000. Everything else becomes noise.
What I changed: New task, new chat. No exceptions.
The result? Faster responses. Sharper reasoning. The model actually remembers what we’re talking about — because there’s less to remember.
Why “Detailed Explanations” Slow Everything Down
I used to ask: “Explain this code in detail and suggest improvements.”
The model would write 500 words. I’d skim it, grab the one line I needed, ignore the rest.
Then I learned: Output tokens are generated sequentially. One by one. More output = longer wait.
When I ask for “detailed explanation,” I’m not just getting more text. I’m waiting longer for text I don’t need.
What I changed: I started being specific about what I want back.
- “What does this function do? One sentence.”
- “Fix this bug. No explanation, just code.”
- “List the issues. No elaboration.”
Same quality answers. Half the wait time. And the model stays focused on what I actually asked.
The Non-English Penalty
This one surprised me.
Tokenizers are trained mostly on English. English words usually become single tokens. Other languages get split into multiple tokens.
- “Hello” = 1 token
- “Merhaba” = 2–3 tokens
Same meaning. But in a conversation, my Turkish prompts fill up the context window faster than English would.
That means: Faster context bloat. Earlier quality degradation. The “forgetting” problem hits sooner.
What I changed: When possible, I prompt in English and ask for the response in my language. “Explain this error. Reply in Turkish.”
Cleaner context, same result.
Why Expensive Models Sometimes Give Better Results Faster
I used to default to the cheapest, fastest model for everything. Why wait longer or use more quota when the small model can do it?
Here’s what I found: For simple tasks, this works fine. For complex tasks, it backfires.
The cheap model gives me an answer that’s 70% right. I spend 3 more messages correcting it. Each message adds to context. Quality degrades. I end up frustrated with a bloated conversation.
The better model gets it right the first time. One message. Done.
What I changed: I match the model to the task.
- Quick syntax questions, simple fixes → Fast/cheap model
- Architecture decisions, complex refactors → Best model available
Counterintuitive, but using the “slower” model often gets me to the answer faster.
Cursor vs Claude: They’re Optimized for Different Things
I kept switching between tools thinking one was “better.” They’re not better or worse. They solve different problems.
Cursor’s approach:
- Compresses context aggressively
- Shows file structures instead of full content
- Optimized for speed and quick iterations
Great for: Daily coding, autocomplete, small fixes. Breaks down when: Large refactors that need full codebase understanding.
Claude’s approach:
- Uses massive context window (200K tokens)
- Pulls in more files for deeper understanding
- Optimized for comprehension over speed
Great for: Architecture decisions, multi-file changes, complex debugging. Overkill for: Simple questions that need quick answers.
What I changed: I stopped expecting one tool to do everything.
- 80% Cursor for daily work
- 20% Claude for complex problems
The tool-hopping stopped. The frustration stopped. Each tool does what it’s good at.
The Changes That Actually Mattered
After 6 months of learning this:
- New task, new chat — Clean context = sharp responses
- Specific output requests — “Just code, no explanation” = faster answers
- English prompts when possible — Slower context bloat
- Right model for the job — Complex tasks get better models
- Right tool for the job — Stopped expecting one tool to do everything
Same tools. Same subscriptions. Noticeably better output.
Try This Tomorrow
Pick one change. Just one.
My suggestion: For one day, start a new chat for every new task. Even if it feels inefficient.
Watch how much sharper the responses get.
Then next week, try adding “Be brief. No explanations unless asked.” to your prompts.
Small changes. Big difference.
This took me months to figure out. Hope it saves you some time. What’s working for you?
